chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: VIST 477 / VIZA 677 / CSCE 650
Course Title: Virtual Reality
Time: Tuesday/Thursday 8:00 am to 9:40 am
Location: ARCC 414
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: This course covers the following: theory and practice of virtual reality; interactive 3D virtual environments; input/output devices, 3D interaction techniques, augmented reality, role of realism in VR, navigation techniques, design guidelines and evaluation methods.

chunks.title: Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in VIST 272, CSCE 221, or CSCE 441. Cross Listing: CSCE 446/VIST 477.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
If all the activities and assignments of the class are completed successfully, at the conclusion of this course, you should be able to:

• Produce interactive 3D virtual environments and 3D interaction techniques
• Develop immersive virtual reality applications with a team
• Characterize and describe virtual reality systems in terms of display properties and interaction techniques
• Evaluate virtual reality applications in terms of design, purpose, benefits, and limitations
• Review and organize past and current virtual reality techniques and knowledge
• Analyze the response users have to your developed project
• Identify sources of information outside the classroom that can be helpful for the design problems and technical problems presented in class.
• Analyze, critique, and present work in a productive and effective manner using appropriate terminology.

chunks.title: Textbook / Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Reading and viewing assignments will be given from articles and research papers that are available through the university. There is no required textbook for the course, but the recommended textbook will complement the topics covered in the course.

• 3D User Interfaces: Theory and Practice (2nd Edition). Joseph J. LaViola Jr., Ernst Kruijff, Ryan P. McMahan, Doug Bowman, Ivan P. Poupyrev. ISBN-10: 0134034325.

chunks.title: Course Structure
chunks.content:
Different topics of Virtual Reality will be detailed in class activities and lectures. In-class programming exercises on specific aspects of the design process of Virtual Reality applications will be given. Quizzes will be used to evaluate proficiency in the class's contents. A semester-long programming assignment, divided into six programming checkpoints. Each programming checkpoint will require students to obtain user evaluations of the current state of their application. There will be no exams for this class.

Programming Assignments – VR Interaction Technique
A semester-long project will be assigned in which students will use the concepts learned in class to create a Virtual Reality application. The project will be developed through six incremental programming assignments. The objective of these assignments is for the students to familiarize themselves with the pipeline to create Virtual Reality applications through various methods. The instructor will provide a detailed rubric and specifications for the assignments during class. These assignments will be evaluated through fully functional programming solutions, deployed in Oculus Quest devices. These assignments will be performed in teams of two or three students, with no exceptions.

Progress Evaluation
Each Programming Assignments will be accompanied by a progress evaluation session. Students will share their developed application with the rest of the class so others can conduct a series of evaluations related to the application's usability, user experience, and overall enjoyment. The objective of these evaluations is not only to assess the applications, but to assess the students' ability to conduct a thorough user evaluation in the context of Virtual Reality. Students will be evaluated based on the quality of their reviews of the other students' applications.

In-Class Programming Sessions
Each week, in-class hands-on programming sessions will be held. Prior to the sessions, a video will be uploaded to familiarize students with the content. Students are expected to watch and implement the content shown in the video before coming to class. During class, the programming exercises will be assigned to elaborate on the assigned videos. These exercises will provide small challenges or highlight potential uses of the topics covered in the videos. Students are expected to complete the exercises during class or by the end of the day, at the professor's discretion.

Research Documentation
Students will produce a formal research paper documenting their semester-long VR project, focusing on the novel interaction technique developed throughout the six programming assignments. This documentation should follow the format and structure of a professional academic conference or journal paper in the fields of Virtual Reality, Human-Computer Interaction, or Computer Graphics. Students may choose any relevant conference or journal template (subject to instructor approval) and must include standard sections: Abstract, Introduction, Related Work, Design and Implementation, User Evaluation, Discussion, Conclusion and Future Work, and References. The paper should be 3-5 pages (excluding references), include figures or diagrams illustrating the interaction technique, cite at least 10 peer-reviewed academic sources, and demonstrate equal contribution from all team members.

XR Showcase / Viz-a-Gogo Presentation
Students will present their applications at one of the Visualization end-of-the-year student-work presentations: the XR Showcase or Viz-a-Gogo. Students will create a trailer video to showcase their application. Afterwards, users visiting the students' stand should be able to wear an VR headset to demo the application and provide appropriate feedback.

chunks.title: Grading and Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Course Activities
| Course Activities | Weight |
|---|---|
| Programming Assignments | 60 |
| Progress Assignment #1 | 10 |
| Progress Assignment #2 | 10 |
| Progress Assignment #3 | 10 |
| Progress Assignment #4 | 10 |
| Progress Assignment #5 | 10 |
| Progress Assignment #6 | 10 |
| Progress Evaluations | 18 |
| Progress Evaluation #1 | 3 |
| Progress Evaluation #2 | 3 |
| Progress Evaluation #3 | 3 |
| Progress Evaluation #4 | 3 |
| Progress Evaluation #5 | 3 |
| Progress Evaluation #6 | 3 |
| Research Documentation | 8 |
| In-Class Programming Sessions | 5 |
| XR Showcase / VIZ-a-Gogo Presentation | 9 |

To aid in understanding the method by which your assignments are evaluated, read the following description carefully. The actual grading criteria will be related to these categories but will be more specific in nature.
Letter grades will be assigned according to the following guideline:
A = 90-100 (Excellent, Exceptional works)
B = 80-89 (Good, Average works)
C = 70-79 (Satisfactory, but works require improvements)
D = 60-69 (Passing)
F = 59 and below (Failing)
I=Incomplete.

Course grades will be calculated based on a combination of weighted scores for projects, assignments, participations, and exams. For the semester, the total grade is based on 100 points, which is equal to 100% of your grade.

Letter grades will be assigned according to the following guideline:
A = 90-100 (Excellent)
B = 80-89 (Good)
C = 70-79 (Satisfactory)
D = 60-69 (Passing)
F = 59 and below (Failing)
I=Incomplete.

To aid in understanding the method by which your project assignments are evaluated, read the following carefully. The actual grading criteria will be related to these categories but will be more specific in nature.

F: 59 and below: The student work is unresolved; the intentions are unclear and major criteria or goals lack resolution. Presentation is incomplete and/or of poor quality. There is a complete lack of problem solving, creative content and/or technical merit.

D: 60 -69: The solution has problems in two or more major areas; skill and design development is marginal or incomplete. The project lacks imagination and/or design potential.

C: 70 -79: The student has solved the problem, but the solution lacks depth of understanding; Some program goals are not completely satisfied and the solution contains little promise even though most issues have been addressed.

B: 80 -89: The student work shows imagination and potential. Presentation and design content is good; program requirements are fulfilled but in need of more refinement or development. There are no major issues that would require a total redesign of the project.

A: 90- 100: The student work has imagination and the solutions to the problems show understanding and thought. The problem solution is highly developed and well presented; the entire assignment shows depth and breadth and is well coordinated. The assignment's potential has been achieved.

chunks.title: Extra Credits
chunks.content: Opportunities to earn extra credit are not promised, but the instructor may opt to offer supplemental assignments for extra credit. Details will be determined per assignment and must be agreed upon by both the instructor and the student.

chunks.title: Teamwork Policy
chunks.content: Teamwork is an integral part of this course. The instructor would like to reward those people that show exceptional team dynamics and correct those who do not. The instructor will provide a single-sheet peer evaluation form that you will use to evaluate the participation and behavior of your teammate. In case of repeated reported team misconduct, the instructor will hold a meeting with the team to address the problem, in a case-by-case fashion.

chunks.title: Graded Attendance and Participation
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments. Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
Your grade will be based upon attendance and active participation in a wide range of class activities. You are expected to be in class from start to finish every day that we have class, (There are exceptions such as health, please see the University Attendance policy: https://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07/).
It is the student's responsibility to inform the instructor of any intended absences.

Excused absences: A list of excused absences can be found in Student Rule 7.1 (http://studentrules.tamu.edu/rule07). Except for absences due to religious Holidays, the student must notify his or her instructor in writing (acknowledged email message is acceptable) prior to the date of absence if such notification is feasible. In cases where advance notification is not feasible (e.g. accident, or emergency) the student must provide notification by the end of the second working day after the absence. This notification should include an explanation of why notice could not be sent prior to the class. If the absence is excused, the instructor must either provide the student with an opportunity to make up any quiz, exam or other graded activities or provide a satisfactory alternative to be completed within 30 calendar days from the last day of the absence.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• Late work will have 10 points deducted for every day that it is late.
• Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy. (See Student Rule 7.)
• Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.
• Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
• Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor. (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).
• The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an unexcused absence. (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).
• Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Use of AI in the Classroom
chunks.content: AI tools are allowed to generate content (text, video, audio, images) for any work (assignments, activities, responses, etc). Since those will be part of your evaluation in this course, any student work submitted using AI tools should clearly indicate what work is the student's work and what part is generated by the AI. When AI is being used for class projects and/or assignments, students MUST cite which AI application they used, and describe how it was used in their assignment. If any part of this is confusing or uncertain, please reach out to your instructor for a conversation before submitting your work.
